Name: 2_math_calculus_algebra_linear
Representative_Docs: ['for general programming i d say arithmetic and basic algebra it helps if you know how to solve basic algebraic equations understand what the log function is understand on a basic level what linear and quadratic equations are i don t think you need much beyond this for most day to day programming the hard part of programming usually isn t mathematical in nature it s more stuff like figuring out what exactly is valuable to build how to structure code in a way that s clean and maintainable figuring out how to coordinate and plan projects for people interested in exploring the more theoretical aspects of computer science or improving their overall foundations i d recommend discrete math with a specific focus on learning how to write proofs specific topics worth exploring in more depth include propositional logic various proof techniques e g proof by induction maybe some basic set theory and maybe some automata theory regular expressions finite state machines you won t directly apply many of these topics for most software engineering jobs but they ask you to develop the same kind of thinking you need to write computer programs for example writing a proof has some deep structural similarities to writing a function you can kind of think of every function header and docstring as a theorem you want to prove and the function body as the proof that it s possible to implement the behavior described in the docstring for people interested in game development or computer graphics in general linear algebra basically learning how to manipulate vectors and matrices of numbers it turns out lots of graphics related tasks involve running the same operation again and again on multiple inputs think running the same shader on every pixel you want to render or whatever linear algebra is basically the mathematics of manipulating lots of numbers at once so the two fields go nicely together as a bonus if you can represent your logic as a linear equation you can speed it up by running it on a gpu a gpu is basically just a device custom built for doing linear algebra efficiently for people interested in machine learning nlp or other ai oriented fields linear algebra statistics and calculus machine learning also involves lots of number crunching which is why linear algebra is useful modern day machine learning is also very statistical in nature for example a neural network for detecting birds in images works by return the probability the image contains a bird rather then a yes or no answer calculus is a core prerequisite for more advanced statistics concepts for people interested in cryptography discrete math with a specific focus on number theory number theory is basically the study of what we can do with integers whole numbers it also turns out lots of modern day cryptography hinge around doing clever things with integers you can get a surprising amount of leverage by combining prime numbers along with basic operations such as modulus multiplication and exponentiation', 'i assume you know basic arithmetics algebra amp geometry discrete math is by far the most important since cs is essentially applied discrete math this includes logic combinatorics set theory number theory etc the good news is that it s actually not that hard i suggest the book by susanna epp it s the opposite of dry calculus is also taught and may be used indirectly for discrete math or directly in graphics algorithm analysis and a couple others i may be a minority here but in my humble experience calculus hasn t been that important for pure cs at least compared to electrical engineering for graphics you especially want to study linear algebra it s also used a lot in ai and machine learning for relational databases you should know relational algebra from my experience it is really easy to understand at least for the level you ll want to achieve in cs for lower level computing boolean algebra amp calculus are your friends the math you should know is determined by what you want to do except for discrete math if you are not interested in ai or graphics there s not much of a reason to learn linear algebra likewise there s no reason to learn relational algebra if you re not interested in databases that said studying it will only enhance your understanding of computing and can only make you a better programmer if you re trying to self study cs you ll have to force yourself to learn them even if you re not interested they are included in most cs curriculums also you don t have to master any of these you ll just want to have good foundations the more you know the better', 'math is very important for certain disciplines in cs especially if you are doing research calculus and linear algebra are very useful for graphics and computer vision i took a graphics course in college as an elective and it was very heavy into math and physics cause we were doing animation and graphics by hand but a lot of jobs will never use much math tbh basic ideas in algebra can be helpful and discrete math is helpful too i took tons of math during my degree because i enjoyed it and i think the concept of math and how you do math might be more helpful for programming than actually the math that you do if that makes sense']

Name: 4_web_dev_development_web dev
Representative_Docs: ['gt what else is there besides web development software engineering in a more general sense that means developing systems that aren t related to web applications aerospace embedded systems operating systems machine learning defense logistics enterprise software consumer software cybersecurity cryptography industrial systems manufacturing and mechanical systems writing software for a power plant or cars and planes and drones or software ecosystems that power day to day work and infrastructure in large companies the list goes on forever gt what is web development web development is software development pertaining to websites and web applications that could mean anything from the web pages you re looking at now to the underlying systems that power them such as how data flows through them the processing and number crunching to produce that data etc also worth mentioning app development is a sister to web development these days because of the overlapping ecosystems and user space gt why is it so popular two big reasons because it s in your face everywhere it s the first thing people think of because it s what most average people and developers alike spend most of their time interacting with in a day because it s much easier to break into the space and start as a beginner or even find a good job without an engineering degree you don t need the same proficiencies in mathematics physics computational theory and understanding of low level hardware for web development that you do for things like aerospace embedded systems cybersecurity etc web development is also much easier to freelance in and learn quickly because of the existing ecosystem of open source frameworks and tools also web development typically takes more manpower than other niches so there are just a lot of web developers in comparison to other niches', 'sure there are a lot of potential development specialties so i ll cover some of the most common most development nowadays is consumer or business applications deployed to web or mobile in that environment you have the following specialties frontend specializing in development of uis in the browser the core skills here are javascript typescript css and javascript frameworks react angular vue the key here is getting to know the web environment really well truly understanding web layout styling the js engine and application state management you also need a basic understanding of backend technologies to be able to interface with the server but can get away with not being an expert most frontend devs also have some amount of design ux skill as they are typically the ones working most closely with designers to ensure a good user experience backend specializing in development of the server side data processing that powers applications a backend dev would typically know several backend languages java go c python ruby etc with their corresponding web service frameworks spring asp net rails etc you also need strong database expertise knowledge of deployment tools docker kubernetes etc and at higher seniority distributed systems design fullstack a very controversial term in general it means someone who s competent both frontend and backend but what competent means varies wildly from company to company at some companies this is frontend who can also write a crud endpoint at others it s backend but could add a simple button at others it s real cross hitting there s also a general frustration among engineers with companies expecting people to know everything which often leads to people who really don t know what they are doing cobbling things together devops responsible for building and maintaining the tools that allow you to build and deploy your code and keep it running in productions key skills are ci cd tools like jenkins deep knowledge of cloud platforms like aws and azure deep knowledge of container technology like docker and kubernetes networking and usually a bit of infosec knowledge also needs to know underlying tools like operating systems and databases well usually fluent in scripting languages like python has a lot of overlap with backend often a little better paid because of supply demand issues note that devops generally also has much more time on call than other specialties so be ready to be paged at am mobile exactly what it sounds like builds mobile apps can either specialize in android or ios or do both like frontend often needs a fair amount of ux design skills key skills are all about mobile platforms swift and or kotlin java and the huge toolkits that support mobile development generally higher paid again for supply demand reasons qa test engineering a role that varies a ton by company at some companies it doesn t exist at others it s a manual testing role that s a second class citizen and at others it s a full specialty focused on the development of testing tools and processes tools engineering you build tools that help other developers do their job better generally only exists at large companies engineers can overlap heavily with devops database administration generally exists either at older companies or bigger ones is all databases all the time with a focus on performance and security security super varied by company at some places this is essentially a devops specialty focused on security including penetration testing security auditing security practices and processes and of course incident response at other places it s a non dev role if you go into this field make sure you know exactly what kind of work you want to do and find a company thay has those sorts of roles business intelligence data analysis focusing on reporting key skills are sql data warehouse technologies and various bi tools tableau amplitude etc not really a development role per se but adjacent data engineering concerned with getting data especially analytics data into the right place often responsible for building data warehouses key technologies include databases kafka and message queues data warehouse tech like redshift and big data tools like the hadoop ecosystem data science depending on the company this is either something done by people with real degrees in statistics and or mathematics or it s a fancy word for a bi analyst it is a major problem that companies hire experts in statistical modeling and or machine learning and then basically have them run reports as a self taught person this field isn t really open to you additionally there are developers who build things other than standard web applications including the folks who build operating systems desktop software embedded systems and a million other things these specialties are a lot less accessible to self taught folks since they generally require knowledge of lower level languages and systems', 'i would recommend to narrow down your preferred field of interest just like how there are different types of doctors programming has different areas each with their own niche each of those fields requires different programming languages you should first determine your area of interest and then find which programming language is best suited for that area here are some fields that could perhaps be useful in making up your mind note that i could not possibly list every single field as there are too many what i can do is give you an overview for you to do more research to find what interests you the most web development if you have an interest in making websites web apps like facebook netflix or even a simple small business website etc than this field would be for you the ecosystem is large and always evolving jobs are readily available but so is competition the good thing is that starting in this field is easy due to the huge amount of learning resources online if this is the case the javascript can be your starting point do note that web dev can further be specialized into areas such as front end development back end development or even full stack development do note that web dev can extend into other areas such as mobile dev more info mobile development the demand for mobile app is huge almost everything has an app these days from apps like uber to mobile games the opportunity is huge in this sector similar to web dev depending on which area to get into the languages i recommend are java android app swift ios c c mobile games do note that mobile game dev is its own beast to tackle more info data science if you are more academically inclined data science can be a field that interest you here python reigns supreme good thing about python is that even if you are not interested in data science this language can be a good entre point to learn concepts of programming do note that there can be many niche fields within this field such as machine learning ai statistic and data visualisation note that while this field can pay a lot of money it also involves a lot of math so if you are not too mathematically inclined this might be hard to get into more info cloud computing if you have an interest for networks servers and analysis cloud computing might be your field of interest with a lot of things going to the cloud this field is ever growing esp in the near future here you will work with things like amazon web services microsoft azure and google cloud java can be a good starting language for this field game development mobile console desktop if you have a passion for games then being a game dev can be rewarding do note that people tend to underestimate the work needed in this field game dev is tough but everything is doable if you have a passion for making games the dominant languages in this field would be c and c you will learn things like unreal engines unity and etc more info']

Name: 6_ml_phd_research_ai ml
Representative_Docs: ['this is an automated service intended to preserve the original text of the post some people say it s much harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs while others say there aren t much difference between internationals and domestic in phd admission at least in machine learning those who say it s much harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs claim that international students cannot apply for fellowship programs and also they cannot get the security clearance and may have problems with export control moreover they some programs have hard quota on international student enrollment but those who say citizenship does not play big role in machine learning phd admission claims that funding is not a problem for ml phd programs because generally ml phd programs have a lot of money so they can provide funding for students who do not come with external funding also they say phd students generally do not work on classified projects and international students can get security clearance for some projects finally they say there are not hard quota on international students i am really confused whose saying the truth do you think it s harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs i am a bot and this action was performed automatically please contact the moderators of this subreddit message compose to r askprofessors if you have any questions or concerns', 'some people say it s much harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs while others say there aren t much difference between internationals and domestic in phd admission at least in machine learning those who say it s much harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs claim that international students cannot apply for fellowship programs and also they cannot get the security clearance and may have problems with export control moreover they some programs have hard quota on international student enrollment but those who say citizenship does not play big role in machine learning phd admission claims that funding is not a problem for ml phd programs because generally ml phd programs have a lot of money so they can provide funding for students who do not come with external funding also they say phd students generally do not work on classified projects and international students can get security clearance for some projects finally they say there are not hard quota on international students i am really confused whose saying the truth do you think it s harder for international students to get into top ml phd programs', 'the simplest not easiest path to working in ai would be to get a b s in compsci and then a phd in ai ml specifically that takes about ten years so unless you really love the field it doesn t make sense to take that path in addition the field at that level is highly competitive meaning you need years of research experience not just swe internships and multiple published papers as a lead author at reputable conferences in order to have a shot of getting into a top phd program you can get some jobs with only an m s but that still will put you at at least five years of schooling if you do an accelerated b s m s program i ended up getting into ml by joining a startup straight out of college b s in compsci and building my skill set as an mle while being relatively underpaid k salary until we raised then a little over k in the end it worked out because i just got a great offer as an ml engineer at a faang company in the bay for almost triple my last tc however the job is primarily swe tasks building supporting infrastructure for ml tasks in the robotics domain it s not doing any novel ml research if i wanted to do that i d need a phd i m planning on going back to school for an m s in order to learn more about ml mainly just because i enjoy learning about it and because my new company is willing to foot the bill in conclusion there are a few questions you need to ask yourself what do you want to do in ml if you want to develop cutting edge ml methods then you d likely need a phd if you just want to develop ml infrastructure then you d likely need an m s or you could do what i did and learn while being underpaid at a good startup then job hop a year or two later for a x tc bump how much do you like whatever it is you want to do in ml ml research is notoriously difficult in the early stages when you re first learning the field unless you feel like you re driven by the force of god to do it you re likely not gonna be able to keep up with people in what is arguably the most competitive niche market in human history if you just want to develop infrastructure it ll be easier but still tough how can you find a support system for whatever it is you want to do in ml this is really a huge piece of learning about any field a problem that might take you weeks to figure out will take an expert literally seconds whatever it is you decide to do you should try to build community around it to encourage you when you find yourself struggling']

Name: 7_school_schools_berkeley_college
Representative_Docs: ['i hope this doesn t come across as pretentious anything i mainly just want to share my experience apply to college this year in hopes of helping the class of so i would consider myself a pretty solid average above average applicant i m from the sf bay area white hetero male from a pretty wealthy zip code i went to a pretty solid public school i have a w gpa uw and cal a g gpa got a on the sat though if i m being honest i was high half the time i took the practice tests so i prob could ve done better i have taken every high level math class at my school ap stat both ap calc and multi variable i took two years of physics including appcm ap chem ap csp and ap spanish got as in all those classes but got a few bs in english generally speaking transcript wise pretty good i had good ecs too president of a service club buildon where we fundraise money to help build schools in developing countries swam for a few years at my school did various service activities like beach cleanups i ve done a lot of engineering related projects like building an electric skateboard and bike designed a neural network to detect forest fires from areal images and several other projects for science fairs with electronics d printing etc etc i did not hire a college counselor because i did not think i needed one my essays were pretty solid thats what our school college advisors said at least i wanted to study mechanical aerosapce engineering that s what my dad studied and i have always wanted to however most of the top engineering schools are public and being from california i was oos for most this leads me to my first piece of advice if you are planning to study engineering apply to some schools as non engineering in total i applied to schools ucb ucsb ucsd uci ucla usc slo cpp csulb uiuc gt uva mich wisco purdue columbia cornell wake lafayette neu cwru ncsu clemson and vt all except for cornell and wake i applied mechanical or aerospace engineering in total i was waitlisted at of the schools accepted into cpp wisco lafayette csulb nscu clemson rejected from cornell usc gt ucla ucb and mich needless to say i got fucked i was waitlisted at cal poly i genuinly don t understand because cal poly was a school a really thhought i could get into the only two schools i considered attending were wisconsin and lafayette seriously though i regret applying for engineering would i have gotten in to more schools if i applied for something more liberal arts like physics or math maybe will i ever know no but just the idea that it could ve keeps me up at night my second piece of advice would be apply to a shit ton of schools if application fees are not an issue and be catious when applying oos to public schools everything is so unpredictable and appkying oos puts you a pretty decent disadvantage unless you re valedictorian or some shit in my case i wish i applied to more cal state schools like ucd ucr ucsc sjsu and others like santa clara and uo and co mines maybe some as engineering while others probably physics all cal state schools have the same app and all i had to do was check another box i didn t think my biggest regret would be not applying to uc davis but here i am wishing i did anyways everyone i talked to about my college process was pretty shocked i was put on waitlists so far the only one i have been accepted to is virginia tech which i declined because it s in the middle of nowehre columbia uva and ucsd have all said they re relieving me from the waitlist though i don t have muchh hope to get off any other waitlists which really fucking sucks as a california resident i thought i would have a better chance at getting into more state schools but fate decided otherwise i do tend to dwell on the past more than what is healthy so this is partially just a rant but i also thought some juniors could find this informative i wish i knew this information when i applied hence why i am sharing currently i plan on going to uw madison rn which is a great engineering school and im excited for but i nonetheless have my regrets amp x b oh ya also for those of you seeing what people consider safeties in this community take it with a grain of salt a lot of people seem to be out of touch with what is actually considered a safety college prestige is much more nuanced than just their acceptance rate like purdue and Name: 8_writing_write_ai_students
Representative_Docs: ['on the one hand i admire your optimism and we as a species have been through this before like when writing made the need for memorization of epic poems ala homer obsolete and so after a century or so people lost the ability to memorize a word poem or when we invented the word processor for a century people had been forced to organize their thoughts ahead of time by the linear progress of the typewriter but now with the computer paragraphs could be moved whole chapters rearranged etc easy peasy organizational skills were lost but flexibility was gained but this feels different yes writing cost us memorization but it created a whole new way of approaching the world intellectually writing created philosophy history science having a computer write for us does not offer us any new tools it s a crutch a better analogy for ai writing would be google maps gps this pairing doesn t actually add any new ability or new possibility there is almost nothing you can do with google maps gps that you cannot do with just a regular paper map and a compass it s just easier and the ease creates learned helplessness kids today cannot get around without their phones because they ve never learned how but most importantly no new vista is opened for them they we have become more passive consumers not actors presentations in class debates evaluation of discussions verbal exams i think its possible not everyone is going to like it but there are many ways forward here in every one of these writing remains the lynchpin good writing ahead of time is the key to a good presentation though speaking and composure add of course but they are the window dressing a good in class debate requires preparation preparation which centers around writing i ve never know a verbal exam to go well with a student who was not also a good writer writing is fundamentally the foundation of modern thinking about half of our modern progress as a society depends fundamentally upon writing the other half depends upon math but also on non intellectual things that involve tactile muscle memory there are many ways forward here i don t think so to me the obvious effect will be that most students the who cheat and the who simply meet expectations will find themselves in a future classroom where rote performance of ai generated pap will get them their diploma and they will move on to jobs where ai similarly does all the work and they re basically sitting in a chair pushing a button like a monkey but will want something more they ll learn writing go to elite schools that still support it and entrench themselves as the real creative innovators so we ll see a massive escalation of the social class split a new world where the vast majority of the populace does not learn the basical skills to intellectually contribute while a literate elite basically create run and thereby own everything it ll be the new middle ages by which i mean an elite literate world of scribes and scholars who basically are culture and who are or are connected to those who own everything and a mass of functionally illiterate who occupy a social role but do not have even the most basic skills to contribute and thus doomed to forever be a cog in someone else s machine', 'my elearning office had a video demonstration and discussion about the topic of ai in writing a couple of weeks ago and had an excellent discussion with some of our faculty members our extremely tentative conclusions were two fold first a short term reactive approach ai so far is not very good at scaffolding work from one assignment to another so any time you can build writing assignments that build on prior work it s more difficult to rely on ai to produce meaningful content ai so far has no concept of indexicallity it can t refer to external objects in a meaningful way if your writing prompt is based on the annotated bibliography your peers shared last week write about the trends or themes ai has absolutely no idea what to do with a prompt like that ai so far doesn t know how to synthesize knowledge or make inferences if you ask it to write a book report it can absolutely do that if you ask it to compare and contrast two different books it struggles but more interesting i think is the long term unanswered question what do writing skills even look like in a world with ubiquitous ai writing tools math curriculum used to spend time teaching students how to use a slide rule and we don t do that anymore because it s an obsolete skillset spending a ton of time teaching the mechanics and structure of writing might be headed that way as well if humans aren t needed to write mechanically correct and logical sentences then what can humans bring to the table creativity intuition persuasion analysis i would argue that if anyone is equipped to answer those questions it s writing educators', 'my elearning office had a video demonstration and discussion about the topic of ai in writing a couple of weeks ago and had an excellent discussion with some of our faculty members our extremely tentative conclusions were two fold first a short term reactive approach ai so far is not very good at scaffolding work from one assignment to another so any time you can build writing assignments that build on prior work it s more difficult to rely on ai to produce meaningful content topic proposals intro paragraphs drafts and revisions any kind of scaffolding is difficult to fake with ai and it s just generally good teaching ai so far has no concept of indexicallity it can t refer to external objects in a meaningful way if your writing prompt is based on the annotated bibliography your peers shared last week write about the trends or themes ai has absolutely no idea what to do with a prompt like that ai so far doesn t know how to synthesize knowledge or make inferences if you ask it to write a book report it can absolutely do that if you ask it to compare and contrast two different books it struggles but more interesting i think is the long term unanswered question what do writing skills even look like in a world with ubiquitous ai writing tools math curriculum used to spend time teaching students how to use a slide rule and we don t do that anymore because it s an obsolete skillset spending a ton of time teaching the mechanics and structure of writing might be headed that way as well if humans aren t needed to write mechanically correct and logical sentences then what can humans bring to the table creativity intuition persuasion analysis personal voice i would argue that if anyone is equipped to answer those questions it s writing educators']

that you need to be a genius like elon musk to learn programming i hope that i could help someone with all what i ve said', 'this subreddit is a great place to get help when it comes to programming and questions on the topic but a long time problem has persisted involving generic questions self doubt and negativity about one s hopes to learn programming i d like to clear some of these today so that new comers can achieve better help here now obviously new comers don t know what questions are generic but they are looking for help if you re new here s a small list of most questions people ask which are considered generic am i too old to learn programming i ve been stuck at x for a long time should i quit i am too dumb for programming what should i do you can find more of such questions in the faq which has given the most reasonable solutions explanations to each question listed i d like to state some solutions to the problems above in hope that it may help some of you am i too old for programming this is a common question but with a simple answer no you re not too old for programming there are a lot of people here aged even if i recall correctly and all of them are successfully programmers you re never too old for anything if you want to learn programming do it if you want to make a website do it if you want to make the next facebook do it as long as you have hands the compassion to learn and a functioning body you can learn and do anything i ve been stuck at x for a long time should i quit many people learning programming reach such a point maybe once or twice even several times but never quit because something is difficult if you re stuck on a topic and feel like you re not going anywhere try look for an alternative way to understand it there are plenty of resources out there to help you such as youtube the entire internet and even here most people feel this pressure because they think there s a time limit to learning programming but in reality that s not the case programming is supposed to be learnt at your own pace and your pace may mean taking two weeks to understand loops or months trying to use classes and objects take your time because once you understand it you can look back and see how the struggle has helped you i am too dumb for programming what should i do this one is asked from time to time but one thing to know is no one is too dumb for anything if you did an iq test and your result was bellow average it doesn t change the fact that you ve made it this far in life if you really want to learn programming don t let a number on a piece of paper stop you you ma take as long as you want learning programming and it may seem difficult but take it slow and steady ask someone to explain it to you your mind is as smart as any others person s as long as you believe you can do it amp x b one more problem is people asking for help on different tasks or projects but having done very little research when some people have a problem they may instantly turn to this or other subreddits asking for help this is not how solving a problem is done you need to first lookup your problem on the internet where a solution may be available this is how programmers work when we run into a problem we search the web that s why we have google opened while coding if you can t find the solution on the internet then you can finally come and ask for help it s always best to exhaust the ways of finding a solution before resorting to the public thus it s always best to try yourself first in the scenario you want to start working on a large small project but have no idea how to start follow these simple steps identify the end goal of your project break it down into smaller parts continue breaking down the parts until you have manageable problems that cannot be broken down further start solving each problem and link them together that s your project done this process is known as the top down approach it is commonly used for other projects outside of programming but works very well here is an example of how you would break down your project suppose you want to build a game first identify the end goal a platformer game where the player kills enemies coming at him break it down into parts this could be the window player movements enemy ai tile system and images start building each part start with making a window then create the player then the enemy and finally the images link each part into one program start placing the enemies and player on a tiles map draw out their images onto the window an you re done now this is a vague method of doing it since there are a lot more things to consider but i hope it makes sense by breaking down your projects it s more manageable and each step is small and simple thus it will be less difficult to do finally you shouldn t post on this subreddit with the aim of gaining karama people post here seeking help and advice but if you have the intent of gaining karma then you will solve your problems at a slower rate compared to others and if you don t get the karma you where hoping for it could put you down this is all i have to say i hope it has helped some people and motivated them feel free to provide feedback or criticism on anything i might have missed or explained incorrectly have a great day and keep programming']

compensation but just in case d happy friday i ve found lots of useful advice on reddit so i figured i d do my part to contribute to the community a lot of the technical info here might be relevant just for computer science students or tech workers but i think the general compensation discussion is a bit more generalizable to other jobs so feel free to share this to other communities if you think it might be useful elsewhere this ended up being a lot longer than i thought it would be when i started writing this so i ll put this up top so it s clear to people know your worth if you get low ball offers you don t need to accept them interview look around when you don t need to point above is much more difficult to follow if you re desperate question i want to hear your opinions about how much is a reasonable base salary and amount of equity to expect at small startups i feel like i m getting low balled but maybe i just have unrealistic expectations for what early stage startups typically offer i also did lots of research and what i ve found so far at the end of this post i included ranges for equity offers i ve received along with ranges for base salary i also included links to sites that have this info but if you re currently working at a startup have worked at a startup or received an offer from a startup i think it would be useful to start a thread below with rough numbers so other people can also evaluate their offers current compensation and figure out if they re being low balled i also included resources i ve been using to prepare for technical rounds keep in mind that i didn t just cram this into a week period i ve been using these sources little by little over the past years i got tired typing all this out so i ll add anything i forgot or fix any links later lmk if there are any other question you d like me to answer i hope this post helps other people figure out their own career background around yoe with years at a large faang company swe short stint at startup no defined title but did automation data processing whatever the cto wanted etc and another faang offshoot before qa role current and previous faang positions both contract internship was unpaid did not do cs but did do a bs and i just say that almost nobody has asked about college during the interviews so i don t think this matters for me anymore major projects i ve been involved in at work include creating an automation framework to update db after schema update writing scripts to automate slow tasks that would take minutes that now take ish seconds and a crawler variation to do some research for my team all projects i designed and implemented from scratch by myself typical work involves preprocessing text and extracting info from unstructured text occasional rails and ruby work and a small docker app even though i didn t do cs i d say my foundational cs is very solid and i m fairly confident working with problems that involve hash maps binary trees dfs bfs pre in post order tree traversals tries topological sorting and shortest path a bit shaky on negative cycle questions but those didn t come up i m also fairly good at recognizing common dynamic programming problem variations such as knapsack minimizing maximizing values finding x in some window of size k but i get tripped up on problems that require specific algorithms or background i m not sure how to describe my knowledge of string and array problems since there are soooo many different variations of them also not as confident about greedy problems for those of you curious about how many leetcode problems i ve done i m at around ish problems right now but i did these over a span of more than years so it s a pretty leisurely pace average of less than problem a day over years the feedback i m getting seems to be somewhere along the lines of you would pass with flying colors for a junior role but we think you re not quite at a senior level yet which is a bit annoying but i guess i need to hone my problem decomposition skills more i consistently get feedback that i m great at communicating so i think it really is just explaining my solution to problems in a better way interviews mostly interviewing at smaller to mid size companies ranging from ish people a lot of the places to ish people company and the rest are in between i ve been doing all the technical rounds in python since that s the language i m most comfortable with and here s a summary of the technical questions i ve gotten types of questions typical leetcode questions and for most of the questions i got you could get by if you have a solid understanding of hash maps recursion and strings some questions required a good understanding of oop to answer properly e g inheriting from an object provided by a python standard library and adding functionality to it i got questions about concurrency threading i wrote out fully working code for one of them kind of like designing a system to allow a variable number of workers to process tasks from a work queue in parallel and mocked the concurrency part for the other e g think how you would manage logic for events that can read and write to a shared resource i didn t remember how to use the lock provided by the threading module so i had to mock that part most system design rounds have been pretty closely related to the kind of work the team i would be joining does or related to the industry the company is in i had one system design question that was unrelated to the team company but the question was designing a message app for a specific use case so it wasn t something crazy some questions were problems that seemed similar to the type of work i expect the company works on and not necessarily leetcode problems but leetcode definitely helped me structure my thoughts and think of a solution only had question that i thought was a ridiculous question i think this was ridiculous because the company had ish people total and engineers and the work the company was doing is not related to parsing text or any sort of natural language processing so this seemed like a ridiculously challenging question to expect interviewees to answer i got a somewhat brute force solution but it didn t address all the cases did not move past the technical screen behavioral questions are typical questions like why do you want to work here at company x what are things you are looking for in your next role what kind of work are you interested in doing what does success look like for you in years as in what would you like to be e g an architect a manager an engineer a product manager tell me about a time you took the initiative to do something tell me about the most challenging thing you ve worked on tell me about an experience you are proud of what would you say is your biggest weakness and some more behavioral technical questions that are specific to the company industry interview feedback the companies recruiters don t always provide feedback but usually if there is feedback it has been similar to how i think i did e g if i think i did poorly on the coding portion feedback usually is the same if i think i did really well feedback is usually the same the behavior portion seems to have gone well almost everywhere notable exception to this just an anecdote feel free to skip if not interested i had technical rounds back to back and i thought i did poorly on the first and did well on the second the first question was a dynamic programming question and i didn t realize it was a dp question until the very end when i asked the interviewer for feedback this was the one i was convinced i failed the second question was one of the concurrent questions i mentioned before and i thought i did well on this part only question i asked was a clarifying question about if we should use locks or not since the cpython implementation has the gil so we won t have true concurrency and then proceeded with the problem mostly fine there was a bug where i didn t put all the things that i should have locked inside the mock lock i was using but i fixed this when the interviewer poked me about an edge case the feedback for the first interview was along the lines of great communication skills borderline but ultimately the lack of working solution meant or implied no pass which was surprising to me since i thought i did poorly here second round feedback was along the lines of i needed a ton of help to get started and i was like the only major question i asked was about locks gil concurrency but i didn t really ask for help otherwise this was really confusing feedback for me and i wondered if they mixed me up with some other candidate otherwise it seems like i m pretty consistently above the junior level but not always at the expectations for senior roles i think this is pretty good for yoe but a bit annoying since i don t want to accept a new position as a junior engineer when i m already a borderline senior die out amp x b please stop spewing your personal option as standard you are doing phd for the wrong reasons', 'define fascinating if you don t want to stay in academia at least for a little bit doing a phd is probably not the best move that said there are a few fields where it might be the only way to gain the necessary knowledge ai ml cv nlp while the field is overhyped and most of the work boils down to plain old software engineering or data curation there are definitely industry positions where a phd is an asset cryptography information theory signal processing databases compilers i m certainly missing a few other fields but you get the idea if an industry position will benefit from a deep theoretical knowledge and a good understanding of the state of the art research from academia a phd guarantees that you ve just spent years working on it your intuition about industry research right microsoft or google research won t simply hire you because you completed a phd you need to be a Name: 16_degree_self taught_self_taught
Representative_Docs: ['there are two problems first is knowledge if you ve taken a full year cs program it opens the door to a lifelong career in just about any software engineering field you could imagine web apps desktop apps mobile apps games simulations security databases aerospace ai ml networking embedded robotics finance or literally a hundred other things it s a lot more than just knowing how to program it s tons of foundational material everything about how computers work second is getting your foot in the door the entry level is completely saturated right now there are millions of people with no college degree competing over all of the available entry level programming jobs without a degree it s extremely hard to stand out when you have a degree you ll get massively more interviews it doesn t guarantee a job but it guarantees you an opportunity at least is it possible to get a job self taught sure absolutely it s just hard and once you get a job you ll still have a lot less knowledge and experience than cs grads so you ll have a hard time getting ahead but many do it s not impossible just be realistic that it s not an easy route boot camp diplomas aren t worth anything if you want to learn from one great but it s still self taught an interesting thing happens after you have experience after a few years people care less about your degree experience matters more however it really depends on what you want to do apps is no problem but if you want to work on ai ml or robotics there s almost no chance without a degree', 'just be weary of people who think that self taught and cs knowledge are mutually exclusive you can be self taught and study say the core of a cs degree at some university there s plenty of jobs that require no degree but you probably want to move to a tech hub like san francisco or seattle or new york you don t mention what type of software engineering you want to do if you want to work on web applications then just go the self taught route but learn some cs from textbooks or at lest courses on coursera or something i have met self taught people who work in compilers and machine learning without a degree so a degree is not necessary think about this if you get a job you will be a senior engineer or close in years which is the time it takes to get a degree use those years while you work to learn the core of cs degree which is doable', 'gt i feel that if i were to purely self teach i could focus more on fundamentals and raw application spending my time in a more wise way nope you need a degree because you need to learn more than you can being self taught most self taught candidates fail completely on theory and fundamentals in career limiting ways here s the most often missing pieces of education big o or little or omega or theta discrete math statistics multiple languages people s heads explode when they first see a functional language compilers basic hardware and software architecture design patterns advanced algorithms and data structures yea heaps and tries actually have uses anything close to ml ai writing on top what you learn a degree is getting an institution to vouch for you if i see you had a gpa at mit stanford etc i will pick that candidate over someone self taught or from a bootcamp not that i would never hire a self taught or bootcamp grad if you are right out of school vs right out of bootcamp i ll pick right out of a top tier school every time it s a much safer bet that someone who spent years learning full time at a top institution will know cs better than someone doing night classes for weeks a degree will also teach you so much more than just cs you will learn writing critical thinking math physics etc that will round you out and make you so much better than just churning out software if you still want to go the self taught route you will have a very hard time getting your foot in the door once you have years experience being very successful in those years it will even out it s not impossible but it is amazingly harder than going the degree route']

Name: 17_code_ai_software_requirements
Representative_Docs: ['curious what startups you have in mind because code generation from common language instructions is an immensely complicated problem and i personally don t think anybody will be able to nail down a commercially viable code generation technology that s truly general purpose i think you could be right that such technology takes out the bottom say of software jobs but would be surprised if it was that much honestly but if anything because such technology would be most useful for improving the efficiency of human software engineers ai will likely lead to a decrease in demand for software jobs and jobs in general but in software any decrease could also be outpaced be an increase in demand as more and more parts of our life are integrated in highly complex digital systems an assumption you make here is that people will be able to provide sufficiently detailed common language instructions to a code generation ai i think this is a much harder problem than you make it out to be specifying requirements in common language to other humans is hard enough despite the fact that humans have a much more shared understanding of the world of language leaving a key restriction out might for instance result in unintended consequences that defeat the whole purpose of the system you were trying to build also a huge part of software engineering at higher levels specifically at the bleeding edge of technology is to discover problems and there s a lot of art to that that i don t think an ai would be able to replicate code generation might be possible for applications under a certain level of complexity but regardless the fact that we wouldn t be able to understand what such an ai would be doing under the hood would make it difficult to trust an ai to be the sole builder of a complex system we need to have confidence that the ai will not misunderstand our instructions specifically if the applications being built are critical to a company s success or something that people depend on for their well being public utility infrastructure for instance afaik we re a long way from ai that can debug code effectively much less write code from scratch', 'ai will absolutely replace the jobs of software developers in the future there is no doubt about it already we are seeing the use of ai powered code completion tools which analyze patterns in existing code and suggest lines of code that a developer may want to use these tools are only going to get more advanced eventually reaching a point where they can write entire programs on their own in addition to code completion tools ai can also be used to automate the testing and debugging of code by analyzing patterns in successful deployments and using machine learning algorithms ai can streamline the process of deploying code to production reducing the amount of manual work required by developers it is only a matter of time before ai becomes advanced enough to fully understand and navigate complex systems making it capable of taking on the entire role of a software developer there is no task that a human software developer can perform that ai will not be able to do just as well if not better some may argue that software development requires the ability to think creatively and come up with novel solutions to problems but this is a short sighted view ai has already shown itself to be capable of generating creative ideas and coming up with solutions to problems that humans have never even considered the use of ai in the software development industry will inevitably lead to job loss and disruption but this is simply the cost of progress it is important to embrace the advances that ai brings and to adapt to the changing landscape of the industry in conclusion it is an absolute fact that ai will replace the jobs of software developers in the future the use of ai in the software development industry will bring numerous benefits including increased efficiency and the ability to tackle more complex tasks while there may be some initial disruption the adoption of ai in the industry is ultimately inevitable and will lead to a better future for all chatgpt', 'i asked chatgpt to write a convincing rebuttal to your second argument while it is true that ai algorithms have made significant advances in the ability to generate code and assist with certain tasks it is not accurate to say that they will never be able to completely replace human software engineers in fact it is very likely that ai will eventually be able to outperform human software engineers in many areas first of all the argument that software engineering requires human expertise and creativity is misguided while it is true that software engineering involves a wide range of tasks many of these tasks can be automated using ai algorithms for example ai algorithms can be trained to understand requirements design and implement complex systems and test and debug code these algorithms can learn from large amounts of data and can quickly adapt to new challenges and opportunities furthermore the ability to think creatively and critically is not unique to humans ai algorithms can be designed to generate novel solutions to complex problems and they can be trained to evaluate and refine these solutions based on feedback and performance metrics this means that ai algorithms can be just as creative and critical as human software engineers in conclusion it is not accurate to say that ai will never be able to replace human software engineers while ai algorithms may not be able to fully replace human software engineers right now it is very likely that they will be able to do so in the future as ai technology continues to advance it will be able to perform a wider range of tasks and will become increasingly adept at solving complex problems this will enable ai algorithms to outperform human software engineers in many areas and it is only a matter of time before this happens']

Name: 18_data_data scientist_analyst_data science
Representative_Docs: ['what separates a data scientist from others is the data scientists can build predictive models that create value for the employer is quantitative knowledge important yes of course it is however i think there is an overestimation about the level of quantitative knowledge that is required to perform this job which is building predictive models which create value keep in mind this may be different depending on what industry you re in as well i have friends that are data scientists in the finance world that rely a lot more on higher level math then data scientists and other areas the key point i m trying to make is that at least in my experience interviewing for data scientist positions and receiving offers over the past few months none of those positions asked for the high level of mathematics that you discussed those roles certainly exist particularly when we re talking about the folks dealing with what s often called core data science however the majority of data scientist positions being offered today are really more in the branch of data science analytics this is definitely beyond the skill set of a data analyst which was my prior occupation but doesn t quite reach the higher math that you discussed my interview questions were primarily dealing with fundamentals of statistics and probability machine learning models and methods for tuning those models one team i recently interviewed for had tons of folks with higher level math skills they just didn t have someone who have the coding skills along with a statistical knowledge in the end what i m trying to say is that while your definition of what is necessary for data scientists may be interesting it s just not what most of the market is asking for right now and if you re out there job hunting it s best to listen to what the market wants i know in my own organization data scientists without that higher level math are delivering quantifiable value in the predictive models that they develop that are saving my company millions of dollars', 'there are two career paths where the role is referred to as data scientist and you are noticing the gulf between them some people get a bachelor s or master s degree and or self teach and work their way up from data analyst roles to become data scientists they get jobs at small to medium size companies and spend a lot of time data wrangling building dashboards and possible doing some a b testing other people get phds in a quantitative field then go to work as a data scientist at a large company these people write research papers look for ways to use machine learning to improve user experience and do sophisticated a b testing at scale if you are in the first category it can be very hard to switch to the second without a phd it s possible but you will need to do some very in depth project work demonstrate that you are comfortable with complex math and get lucky source i used to work as a data analyst on an analytics team at a consulting company then i worked as a data engineer at a faang company and now i work as a data engineer within the data science org at another large tech company i had a coworker who tried to switch from data engineer to data scientist at the faang company they grilled him on the math behind his master s thesis and he ended up failing the interview', 'incoming wall of text data scientist here roles titles and duties vary across orgs and many people dip there feet in multiple areas for example many ds also do some data engineering work here s how i would define all of this stuff but you will find different orgs divide things differently combine roles etc data analyst most familiar with interpreting and presenting data with respect to business needs and impact usually proficient with sql advanced excel and dashboard software actual modeling skills are minimal to none background usually involves math cs but you will see technical people with a business background people with clinical medical data backgrounds etc doing this work these people are the closest to the business side of things out of all data ml people data scientist modeling is the biggest difference between data scientists and analysts imo able to interpret business problems alongside data and provide models and advanced analysis to deliver business value can provide analytics that require more advanced methods than what an analyst would typically deliver for example making dimensional visualizations of clusters where the original datapoints are from a much higher dimensional space a bit more distance from the business side of things but still very well versed in the business problems at present and solutions usually has a background in cs math physics etc and grad degrees are very common generally uses python and all the data ml packages sql spark here and there cloud mlservices such as sagemaker etc works with a lot of tabular data as well as some nlp and computer vision stuff machine learning scientist think the cutting edge of machine learning stuff that is currently transitioning from academia to industry think deepmind google brain microsoft research etc these people are usually building deep learning models with custom architecture where as a data scientist using deep learning will be more likely to use off the shelf models phd s are incredibly common here as is research experience publications etc definitely not business people most orgs don t need these people data engineer infrastructure is the name of the game usually a cs background with a much higher skill level in cloud data tools cloud ml tools as they apply to building pipelines efficiently storing data hpc clusters ops work etc they take the models built by data scientists and scale the hell out of them make sure everything is bullet proof etc basically data oriented software engineers machine learning engineer this title can really overlap with the data engineer stuff but there isa level of complexity when it comes to optimizing and scaling advanced machine learning algorithms where you need a specialist think how can i combine all the algorithms used to control a self driving car so they work together efficiently don t have bottlenecks work in a live manner don t fail because people die when that happens and all fit into a single computer in a car efficiently managing memory and stuff or how do i configure this big compute cluster using giant custom deep learning models that can t fit on a single gpu to run a bunch of models with massive datasets to solve this genetic research problem that s never been done before most orgs don t need these people']

Representative_Docs: ['you have an excellent resume but i do have some suggestions i d recommend removing the summary section of your resume it doesn t really add anything that can t be seen elsewhere in your resume worse still imo it screams i need to take up space because i don t have enough content to put in my resume in my experience most interviewers glaze right past most summary sections in resumes if you are concerned that you don t have enough content to put in your resume don t be you ll totally fill up your resume if you turn your fake bullet points into real bullet points bullet points should be concise and express one distinct idea the advantage of bullet points is that they re friendlier to read than i wall of text your resume has bullet points but the content of your bullet points are occasionally walls of text for example i would recommend turning this single bullet point developed automation feature using a random forest machine learning model feature resulted in an estimated reduction in user workload used weka to train the model and implemented it into a java application using the weka library into two separate bullet points developed automation feature using a random forest machine learning model resulting in reduction in user workload used weka to train the model and implemented it into a java application using the weka library your single discord of people if it was more than people on your team and i d mention the place if you were top three or there was a lot of teams and your team scored relatively well in which cause i d mention placing kth out of n teams software engineer intern adding food and exercise data is helpful for context but i m not sure retrieving saving updating and deleting is that useful to list out because that s Name: 23_machine learning_machine_learning_population
Representative_Docs: ['in the post he does state that only one part of the tool not sure what it does since i m not a civil engineer uses actual deep learning techniques in general i think it s important to remember that there aren t only neural networks despite that being what most people think of machine learning is a much bigger field that technically covers things as simple as a least squares linear approximation of data clustering methods such as dbscan or tools for categorizing data like support vector machines deep neural networks are only one of many subsets and even within that sphere there is a lot of variation broadly saying ml techniques are inefficient at something sounds a little dismissive if i may say so an example it s true that throwing a deep neural network approach at something like specing a pressure vessel something i m more familiar with but i imagine similar to some of the sizing specing tasks civils do routinely is likely overkill and won t lead to good results however the task does involve a lot of iterating because of co dependencies in influencing factors so using a set of known solutions to interpolate i e a surrogate model is much more efficient albeit less precise and it s arguably a targeted form of machine learning especially if the goal is a quick cost estimate and not the final design this would make perfect sense to me no one in their right mind would or should suggest that some guessed solution is the final one it s a starting point for precise analytical solutions ops tool does not strike me as something that is supposed to give be all end all solutions it just looks like a great estimator for routine tasks and there is always a need for those', 'what is machine learning', 'here s one from statistics in statistics one of the main problems we tackle is trying to learn about some aspect of a population under the caveat is that when we do experiments we only get to see a small number of people or mice or dice rolls or whatever from that population one of the ways we describe how hard it is to learn about a particular aspect of a particular population is by describing how much our best guess at the answer changes as we do more and more experiments if our answer doesn t change very much that s an easy problem if our answer changes wildly between experiments that s a hard problem one of the main drivers of how hard a problem is is how much background knowledge we have about the population we re studying the more prior information we have the easier the problem is for example maybe we want to know about iq scores amongst american kids between and years old and maybe from many previous studies we know that this is basically a bell shaped curve but we re not sure where the center of that curve is that s a lot of prior knowledge we know everything there is to know about iq other than one number that one unknown number is what we call a parameter because there is only one unknown parameter in general we would probably guess that this is a relatively easy problem in fancy statistics speak we call this a univariate parametric estimation problem other problems are much harder where we have essentially no background knowledge about the population we re studying in this case it s not just one parameter that is unknown there are possibly an infinite number of unknown parameters this would seem at face value to be a much more difficult problem we have to use the data we have to learn not just one but essentially an infinite number of things in fancy statistics speak we call this an infinite dimensional estimation problem or nonparametric or semiparametric if you like except that a really smart guy named charles stein showed something cool he argued that you could describe how hard it is to learn about something when you have no background knowledge by describing the hardest possible scenario when you have lots and lots of background knowledge that is some infinite dimensional estimation problems are actually of the same difficulty as really hard univariate estimation problems i think both sides of this story are pretty interesting it s neat that we actually can answer questions when we have essentially no background knowledge of the population we re studying in fact we can do it as well as if we knew almost everything about that population this has paved the way for amazing advances in how we use machine learning to analyze data and solve problems across lots of domains more esoterically it s pretty interesting that i can set up a scenario where you have all the knowledge about a population except for a single number but in terms of answering the question that you re interested in you are no better off than if you had no background knowledge whatsoever']

Name: 24_bootcamp_boot_bootcamps_camp
Representative_Docs: ['boot camps are usually catering only for people who wish to become a web application developer this is my observation so if you go for a boot camp you can miss out a different role that perhaps suits you more meanwhile bachelor studies in computer sciences will prepare you for different paths a data scientist an app developer a web application developer an embedded software developer a game developer an ai developer a robotics engineer etc also boot camps are short term and will not teach you the theory in depth you will become able to develop simpler things but when being faced with complex projects topics then you will be lost you did not mention who you wish to become or what interests you based on that it would be easier to tell if you should go for a boot camp or a bachelor degree in computer sciences do not go for a master degree in computer sciences as your economics degree has not prepared you for computer sciences master studies they will be giving different courses that have pre requirements from bachelor studies it is not too late to go for bachelor degree in computer sciences either i did it when i was we had some guys who were in their s and s as well so you can do fine it is possible to get internships and also junior developer positions even during bachelor studies so you do not need to wait definitely years before applying for a job also you will get all kind of practical experience with different projects during your studies also you will get connections from your course mates and from extracurricular activities people can later on help you in finding a job through your connections you will miss it out when going for a boot camp but then again a boot camp or higher vocational studies in computer sciences will take less time than bachelor studies so you can get hired sooner true you will have less options but you can apply to these less options faster and certification is mainly needed for public procurement projects where the government has to guarantee that the developer has a certain level in knowledge skills or when you are developing software for sectors with critical importance medical sector energy sector nasa etc where a small mistake can cost a lot when developing for regular sectors or working for private companies you will not really need certificates', 'the path that will open the most doors for a lifelong career is a year degree in computer science or possibly a related degree like software engineering it s a long time commitment yes but it will prepare you for anything for the highest chance of success you should plan on doing summer internships after your nd and rd years they pay really well and they dramatically increase your chances of having a job lined up when you graduate you can t just start with zero knowledge attend a boot camp and come out job ready of people who try that end up dropping out or failing out it s just too much to learn in too little time a boot camp can be great if you do about a year of self study and you re successfully building websites and apps and you just use the boot camp as the final icing on the cake to fill in gaps in your knowledge and give you exposure to a wider variety of things but even with a boot camp it s not easy to break into the field and it s especially hard in this economy the entry level market is completely saturated with self taught programmers with or without a boot camp and it s extremely hard to stand out among the competition also boot camps only cover a few specific areas like web development and mobile app development the world of software is huge and tons of interesting things effectively require a degree like games embedded machine learning robotics browsers operating systems distributed systems compilers etc in comparison while a year degree doesn t guarantee you a job it opens the door to all of those fields and you should have relatively little trouble getting interviews at a wide variety of software companies it s still up to you to impress the employer and seal the deal that said programming isn t for everyone if you ve never tried it before i highly recommend you give it a try using free resources to see if you enjoy it before signing up for college or a bootcamp or before spending a lot of money if you love it great but if you hate it you won t waste any time or money', 'definitely a degree is better bootcamps in general prepare you only for a web application developer position some few can prepare also for cyber security specialist or mobile app developer role haven t really seen other fields being represented so a bootcamp limits your knowledge and skills to only one field but university will prepare you for multiple fields as it takes longer another thing with bootcamps is that they often concentrate on the what and how part to get the code written and something done but they don t go that much into theory into the why part so your theoretical knowledge will be much more shallow than it would be when graduating from a college university during degree studies you ll be taught things more in depth the the chances of getting a job itself when you are doing university studies then you get connections from your course mates and also from your professors from a bootcamp you usually won t get that as many of the bootcamps are online and everybody is doing their studies in their own pace and own time no interaction with other students that much in addition to connections university will hold time by time internship job fairs for students so students get a chance to do their internship in one or another company sometimes even get a chance to start their junior developer career in some company companies and universities work together bootcamps don t do that or very few do then your portfolio when you finish your bootcamp your portfolio is generic pretty much the same what everybody else was doing during the bootcamp but can you actually work on projects out of the box that is where a university has its advantage during degree studies there are different coding competitions and hackathons where a person team must come up with some interesting project also the course related projects in general are often more complex than what you are doing during your bootcamp studies and then university wide projects for example my course mates worked on an actual space satellite they developed different software for letting it to fly for communicating with it for tracking it for controlling a ground station etc and now the satellite is actually flying in the space some other students worked on a self driving bus and stuff you will never ever get that from bootcamps so how will you compete with university graduates who have interesting projects to show with your calculator apps and such then group work skills that is needed at work when studying in university you have to do group works time by time when doing bootcamp studies you are doing solo studies and you are missing out code reviews project planning group discussion giving feedback and getting feedback and learning from it yes whoever runs the bootcamp will grade your work but that is not the same as working in a team bootcamps will not introduce you into complex topics either embedded programming applied cryptography machine learning ai bioinformatics medtech robotics signal processing etc complex topics will be covered during university studies']

Name: 25_courses_cs_programming_classes

advanced math though usually not as much as engineers unless it belongs to the engineering school the kind of math most associated with cs is discrete math if you think of most engineering math as related to real numbers or complex numbers which are more or less continuous discrete math often deal with things closer to integers so discrete math usually covers formal logic where you learn basic proofs and then a variety of topics e g set theory number theory combinatorics graph theory circuit theory this is more ee but anyway proof by induction personally i like this kind of math more than calculus do you need that much math for programming mostly no but not everyone is learning to be a web dev indeed many research universities don t teach much web stuff it s not considered research so you wouldn t hire a phd to do that common kinds of courses learn a language for two semesters java c or python learn discrete math learn computer architecture maybe learn assembly maybe not learn languages from other programming paradigms functional declarative oo scripting languages etc this is not for expertise but just to get you familiar with how programming languages differ data
