introverts so it s not their deal oh yeah non cs majors like to make fun of cs majors for being nerds try not to let it bother you', 'i mean in all honesty you just have to look up your own universities cs undergrad curriculum or maybe check out other universities cs curriculum it s mostly open access for any prospective students here are the general areas that i think most or all undergrad cs degrees cover actual math discrete mathematics a mash up course on all essential mathematics that is often needed for cs basics in logic how to proof things set theory and functions combinatorics recursive equations graph theory abstract algebra sometimes includes discrete probability theory too calculus real analysis kind of an obvious one linear algebra besides discrete math one of the most useful math courses ever for a cs undergrad matrices pop up all the time it s not even funny probability and statistics self explenatory all ml enthusiasts better pay attention here theoretical cs these things are all heavily interlinked and are trying to teach you what computation means and how it can be used these courses can be heavily proof based formal languages and automata theory a course on the fundamentals of formal languages grammar and their character computability theory an intro to different computation models like mu recursive functions and turing machines the halting problem and more philosophical stuff too computational complexity theory basically a nintroduction to the different complexity classes and what fits where stuff like np hard problems and np completeness and tons of examples and proofs of why they are what they are programming and algorithms one of the most practical and fundamental part of cs that most people know algorithms and data structures a class that gives you the basic understanding of what typical algorithms are out there how to estimate their complexity etc imperative programming your average c java python intro class functional programming learning what purely functional programming is and stuff like lambda calculus usually either haskell lisp ml whatever other language is out there program verification how to proof that your program is actually formally correct induction proofs hoare logic weakest precondition stuff termination proofs etc concurrent programming a course on how to deal with concurrency computer engineering the actual hardware part of cs where you learn how modern computers work on the physical basis computer architecture a course on digital circuitry cpu architecture memory bus system heavily c and some sort of assembly language operating systems systems programming a course on what os are and what their core task is and what principles are behind them either c or c course internet and computer networks intro to how the internet and protocols works basically either a c or c course usually databases usually a single or two courses on the logic behind relational algebra and modern sql databases typically mysql postgres software engineering a course on software lifecycle practical development models of software project management coding practices and typical design patterns and these things random large project self explenatory and that s basically it of course there are tons of other areas where there are courses but most of them are optional and after doing all your basics every cs student has a free choice on what they want to do machine learning for example is a popular one but like i said courses in ml ar every math heavy and you should be well versed in statistics probability theory and linear algebra', 'there s never been an established way to teach computer science and because it is a technology some stuff has changed over the years most notably many universities no longer teach assembly language at any serious level universities even have some issues about how to classify cs is it math related or engineering related interestingly enough if it s placed in engineering as opposed to arts and sciences or something that is stem but without the engineering then you end up covering more math esp calculus differential equations etc so let s hit the math requirements first most self taught courses the odin project free code camp don t cover much math partly because you can learn programming without doing much math but not everyone doing a cs degree is going to be a web developer some will head to grad school and a lack of math is a huge detriment to advanced cs furthermore because cs came from either math or engineering or got classified that way some math is expected there is i feel a correlation of being decent at high level math and being able to program why is this the case math is hard to learn it has abstractions and so forth if you can figure that out then you can probably figure programming out not saying it s absolutely necessary but cs degrees usually require some math typically this would include two semesters of calculus one course beyond that linear algebra or differential equations with linear algebra being somewhat more useful maybe a stats course if cs was part of engineering you d probably have to do linear algebra and differential equations and multivariable calculus that is you d do all the math that engineering majors have to the math tends to happen in the first two years but some might be pushed to later years for the cs courses it s usually divided into core courses and then elective courses core courses are what most cs majors take and then there are a bunch of elective courses once you reach junior and senior year and are more a hodge podge of different choices typical courses in the core courses cs intro to programming assignment conditionals loops arrays classes cs continuation of programming basic data structures linked lists binary trees binary search trees an advanced tree recursion some basic algorithms e g binary search in an array sorting algorithms discrete math basics of proofs propositional and predicate logic proof by induction weak strong structural examples of different discrete math topics and proving things associated with it combinatorics set theory basic number theory cardinality of infinities cantor s diagonalization proof basic graph theory imo discrete math needs to teach the basics of proofs math majors often take a similar course but it s later in their sequence the three courses above are often taught in the first semesters depending on how much math the students have already had if they haven t reached calculus they need to typically meet prerequisites before taking cs courses these are the early courses when i taught there was a third programming course just to get more programming in then there are the third year courses which can be take second semester second year and first semester third year large universities can offer these courses every semester small ones sometimes only offer it once a year these courses might include computer architecture comparative programming languages languages in paradigms other than that taught in cs and cs e g a functional programming language a scripting language etc numerical computation don t know how common this is these days essentially doing math on a computer software engineering how to build software finally there are often a bunch of courses in junior and senior year and you pick those that fulfill requirements and are interesting to you some examples of courses are ai machine learning data science compilers operating systems databases computer security cyber security computer networks theory of computation numerical analysis advanced algorithms human computer interaction usability etc computer graphics other courses new topics have been added to cs such as data science and cybersecurity so some universities offer tracks where you might focus your course choices to one of these new areas or stick to a general track and pick whatever you like larger cs programs can offer more courses and more often smaller cs program under faculty members will have a more limited choice one huge gap is web development the topics listed above have research interests but web dev is considered purely applied and many researchers do not work in web dev so it isn t always offered some universities do offer it but it s not expected where as data science and security and machine learning had some academic association so many universities started hiring profs for this']

Name: 27_interview_recruiters_candidates_interviews
Representative_Docs: ['turns out you re right according to chatgpt it is unlikely that chatgpt or any other artificial intelligence system will completely replace technical recruiters in the near future while chatgpt and other language models can be very useful tools for automating certain tasks and providing information they are not capable of replicating the full range of skills and abilities that human technical recruiters bring to the table technical recruiters play a crucial role in the hiring process as they are responsible for identifying attracting and evaluating candidates for technical roles within an organization this involves a range of tasks including sourcing candidates through various channels conducting initial screenings and interviews and assessing candidates skills and fit for a particular role in order to effectively perform these tasks technical recruiters must have a deep understanding of the specific technical skills and expertise that are required for a given role as well as the ability to communicate effectively with both candidates and hiring managers they must also have strong interpersonal skills as they often serve as the primary point of contact for candidates throughout the hiring process while chatgpt and other language models can be very useful for automating certain aspects of the recruiting process such as answering common questions or providing information about open positions they are not capable of replicating the full range of skills and abilities that human technical recruiters bring to the table as such it is unlikely that they will completely replace human technical recruiters in the near future', 'i understand the pros and cons of the current software engineering interview paradigm have been beaten to death on this subreddit but i ve seen a lot of posts on here recently arguing against live coding interviews i want to offer an opinion and perspective on why live coding interviews might be a good thing and why changing interviews in the software world to be more like finance law etc might not be as great as you think i have friends in finance law medicine and other engineering disciplines who tell me about what i can only describe as a caste system in their fields you are locked into whichever caste of prestige and pay your education connections and first job place you in and it takes a tremendous amount of effort to move up to the point where it can seem impossible what live coding interviews do for us that is good is that it subverts this caste system doubling your salary by studying interview problems for months is unheard of in other fields self taught people and bootcamp grads working in elite roles at elite companies is nonexistent in other fields it is insane that a job at say google is actually accessible to someone with no connections who was an average student at a state school it s fair to say that these interview questions are nothing like the job it s honestly true you re never going to invert a binary tree building an enterprise product with react and dotnet but faang companies have realized that it has the lowest margin for bullshitting an interviewer can look through your personal projects but at this point how do they know chatgpt didn t just write them for you an interviewer can look through projects you worked on at previous employers but how do they know you re not exaggerating your involvement and contributions to them you may be thinking the answer to these questions is something along the lines of we ll have a conversation about it but that kind of subjectiveness allows for too many false positives they want the binary of they found the optimized solution or they didn t i think of it like the nfl scouting combine they don t throw you in a game or ask you questions about previous games you ve played too many opportunities for flukes or silver tonguing your way into a position you may not deserve they want to see how much you can bench how high you can jump how fast you can run yards etc i ll conclude with two things one i m expecting to see leetcode is just memorization anyone who s really played this game knows how ridiculous this idea is no successful software engineer is memorizing binary search line for line without really understanding it and crossing their fingers it gets asked in their onsite if they did that it would be easy to sniff out but this is also what i mean when i say this style of interview has the lowest margin for bullshitting and not that it s impossible to bullshit some people do get lucky two i do believe it s a bad thing that smaller low paying companies are emulating the hiring processes of faang and adjacent companies they re not considering why these companies interview like that they re following suit in a cargo cult fashion or egotistical enough to think their company is actually on the same level of prestige as faang and adjacent this is really only acceptable when it s a simple problem used to weed out people mass applying with bots or who aren t serious about programming something trivial like return the max element in the provided array', worthless other times i was motivated and encouraged to not give up thank you to all the people that provided me with a positive experience here i wish you all the best of luck and i encourage you to never give up it s so cliche but i can t tell you how many times i cried my self to sleep and how depressed and burnt out i came i never gave up though and i finally get rewarded for it please feel free to dm me if you d like so i can share my personal experience tips with you it might just help you edit adding my resume for reference also check out my post history so you can relate amp amp amp know you re not alone you can also use it as further reference i had some solid folks respond to a lot of those with great feedback tips and advice edit i figured i may as well post my process since i have been dming a few folks i know this is my anecdote and personal experience so take it with a grain of salt i am not claiming to be an expert and if i didn t get this opportunity god knows i would have probably have more months of unemployment it s really difficult out here i can only say that i switched up a few things in these last couple months that dramatically improved my results always be actively applying when i first started i waited for like weeks to begin applying right after graduation last may this is a big mistake if you aren t actively interviewing then you should be actively applying constantly i repeated the same mistake over and over i d apply for a week straight hear little to nothing back and grind for a straight month udemy side projects lc with no apps in between ultimately this made me loose so many good opportunities e g applying to a good position weeks after it s been posted your resume goes a loonnnnngggggg way it needs to portray who you are what you re good at and what you re looking for i had a version of my resume that i went through apps with responses no joke zero responses i posted it on a couple different places to get feedback and advice and that dramatically changed my results the content was good the delivery was poor also try using free ats systems to scan your resume and get some automated ai feed back that the companies use to sort potential candidates from bad ones don t obsess over one thing too much i would only do lc for a couple weeks only and then do udemy for a couple weeks and then do other stuff it was all isolated good recipe for burn out due to lack of variety leetcode is its own beast to give adequate useful advice is tough and different for everyone but to preface i m not good at it all i only just started attempting mediums problems total i got insanely lucky however that the one and only lc medium i was asked i had done a few weeks before other than that really don t obsess too much over lc don t stay stuck on any one problem too long and remember quality over quantity all the way dm recruiters on linkedin ask to have a quick chat about the role and that you are interested to see if you could be a good fit or not don t say that you are a good fit say that you would like to see if you can be this is essential it s todays version of cutting in line professionally of course trust me i got wayyyy better results when i actually started talking to people in the company for quick chats versus sending a cold app and hoping you get a call back amongst other candidates that are equally if not more qualified than you pace yourself have some willpower and self control and dedicate hours a day if you are able otherwise adjust accordingly to max productivity and after that dont touch it again until the next day leave your weekends to your stress outlets and don t focus too much on the grind you need to give yourself a mental and physical break you avoid burning out like this not every technical interview is a lc one i had several interviews where i was asked general questions on oop good front end practices explaining differences between two technologies etc you have to be prepared for those as well cus it sounds bad to say i don t know in fact painting anything in a negative way is not good always try to find a way to make something bad sound good an example when i was asked a question in interviews that i wasn t sure how to answer i wouldn t say i don t know instead i would say something like ah that s a good question i recently started learning about that so i will try to answer my best behavioral interviews matter alot i passed a technical round but was shot down in the behavioral cus i couldn t think of a good response for one of their questions don t wait to be in a ready state to apply it doesn t matter how many interviews i did how many lc problems i solved i never felt ready just go for it take every single interview you can use each one as practice even if you don t want the job just do it you ll find you re less nervous anyway for the jobs you don t want you ll ultimately want to simulate that feeling of not caring and confidence in a real interview again everyone s path is different and i m simply sharing my own in hopes that it could help you so what i m telling you may or may not work everyone s different remember it truly is a numbers game and the field is incredibllyyyyyy over saturated at the entry level best of luck to you all', 'is this the right subreddit or does this need to go somewhere else what would you do if you were in my shoes in terms of the most effective next step s to make any sort of measurable progress continue to do the things i have already been doing modifications to them something i m not doing that i should be give it the heave ho again and do something entirely different summary of education experience at the end of post can provide more specifics depending on what further input information the hive mind requests what i ve been doing which has not been working sending off applications into the black hole networking with existing linkedin connections seeking new ones etc respond to almost all recruiter inbound inquiries opportunities attended virtual job fairs now looking at in person job fairs opentowork posts several times over the period i ve been looking that don t seem to get any attention traction the ones i do see get lots of attention go something like been looking for years etc etc i m about to lose the roof over my head in two weeks resume reviewed revised by colleagues friends who are hiring managers resume reviewed and revised by multiple hr career advisor types resume reviewed in terms of both ats and human readability have consulted with coaches to try and get yet another pair of eyes on the problem who after spending some time to get the background story and check over all the boxes are just as puzzled as i am as to how with my work history education etc and all the things i ve tried done it getting a job is not happening there is at least some validation that through our conversations i am not making obvious mistakes in terms talking to the decision makers the few times that i have had an opportunity to do so recently it is like i m in the twilight zone because everyone is saying they can t find it tech people and here i am willing and able to do having done quite a few core it functions over the years but there are no takers as in i can count the phone interviews on one hand and i have been looking for one year and months at this point with no end in sight here are two examples of what i m dealing with i had a recruiter who thought i was a good fit and was presenting my resume to a hiring manager for a position natural language processing the hiring manager s feedback was that they were not even interested in having a brief phone conversation to chat with me the recruiter told me that the hiring manager thought that since i haven t written code as my primary occupation in the last couple of years i wouldn t be able to do it again i reinforced that just because i was running my business i didn t outsource any it functions for it ranging from running maintaining my own local it infrastructure operating my own web hosting constructing and maintaining our website writing embedded arduino code data analysis gis work etc i had such a case of imposter syndrome from that one potential that i felt like i had to prove that guy wrong the next chance that i could a few weeks later i was asked to do a data engineering exercise as a part of the two interviews that i ve had i should have seen that position was doomed from the start given the task was not just an hour or two thing but they left it totally open in terms of how much time you could spend on it which ended up being a multiple day and back and forth with the team since the requirements were intentionally ambiguous the kicker was that upon completion of the exercise they didn t even seem to have an answer key or way to validate if i had correct results of their because it would have been easy to say you have the correct data output from the input data set provided and met our requirements but instead it s a one hour discussion of the code where half the time some of the committee members are not even remembering their own problem specs my background education all completed at a top tier well respected se usa public university bs biology bs computer science ms information science certificate in bioinformatics certificate in health informatics adjunct faculty at the aforementioned university i started with and tried extensively networking with my prior coworkers there to get me an in somewhere as i am considered an internal applicant but it s either a no without any communication whatsoever or the application sits in the queue never to get even to the first stage certificate in a basic medical career expired volunteered part time to have actual patient care experience and understanding from the end user in terms of having to enter data into an emr as a bonus in terms of working in healthcare informatics as opposed to just being a programmer who has never taken a blood pressure and barely understands human anatomy and physiology work experience years of progressively increasing responsibilities with titles such as it manager systems administrator web administrator developer etc my last role as an employee was in a data engineering science natural language processing role projects from the last job were published in major academic journals i presented work at conferences and gave talks lectured about the work our field in college classes and mentored students i am not an anti social leave me alone to sit behind my monitor programmer i worked with people on problem specs and interacted with c suite hospital executives down to principal investigators and their staff in terms of what i think i should be doing now solutions architect or similar since i am comfortable with and have done sales management project management but not even applying for stuff like that since i can t seem to get more straightforward positions further down the pipeline i left the last position having become tired of work politics and overall lack of opportunity for that next stage promotion to management having literally created my own management opportunities out of thin air my abilities and intellect were typically under utilized i did the work required and did it quickly quicker than my peers i felt like i had to take on more larger projects outside the scope of my job to feel challenged and to try and show initiative toward a potential promotion to management more often than not it was to work on larger scalable projects to benefit the organization and increase my skills at the same time e g deploy systems that would empower enable end users as opposed to having to run menial tasks through employees i had enough and decided to acquire an existing business and left my job for the last years i ran my retail technical electronics small business not it related not healthcare or anything to do with my prior work background i learned a whole new profession in a day transition training from the prior owner and was successful passing most of the failure milestones of a new business since it was new to me and take overs are equally subject to typical business failures i continued to learn improve and go up from there we filled a need people were happy with what we did for them and we had a very high customer satisfaction rating on google yelp and got to be the guys that did the most thorough job and ended up having to fix the work of even the better best competitors in our area on several occasions business got indirectly impacted by the pandemic long story which also unfortunately intersected with a major family health issue as such the business was put on pause to be there as to the question why don t you just go back to your business i still plan to resume it with me being less involved and with it being more like an investment but there are some things that have to be dealt with beforehand that are out my control and the extra dollars in the bank from a normal w would be helpful to straighten some of those things out i figured with all my education skills it would be no problem to find a job to bide my time especially with the current job market but here i am', 'i apologize if i m writing a story i just needed a place to clear my mind and hear the advice of strangers i really don t care if i expose myself i ve been striving to be a more honest person a year ago i was a very narcissistic person and i didn t have the wisdom to know the difference i had a fake it till you make it mentality and i thought it was ok to lie about how competent i was or how much effort i was gonna put into something to get what i wanted i know that a lot of people lie on their resumes and they still get a job where they re doing fine i think this is because sometimes employers just want to see that you re proactive and you might not use all the skills listed also being honest with the fact that they are able to work hours a week is probably the biggest factor last summer i got rejected from a lot of internships i suspect the biggest reason why is because these employers could see through all of my bs being that i was so desperate i ended up emailing some of my professors for internships or just anything to do over the summer one of the professors at my university was kind enough to offer me an opportunity though he said he was interviewing people because too many students were asking him for positions in his research group during that interview the professor and ph d students asked me questions to see if i was a good fit for this internship i remember lying to them about just about everything here are a few i told them i was into competitive programming despite the fact that i ve only participated in competition and solved only leetcode problems and maybe from all the other sites combined i claimed that i didn t care if i were to get an unpaid internship though i actually did i wanted money to at least support myself and i was banking on them perceiving me as humble so they ll give me a paid internship offer reverse psychology i talked about my biggest cs project where i basically worked under another professor at my university and tried to look at runtimes of factoring algorithms and attempted to code up the quadratic sieve i actually copied of the code online and changed approximately lines of code so it works in my favor i flexed and said yea i wrote like lines of code misunderstanding i talked about my knowledge of machine learning algorithms in a class i think because neither the ph d students went to my university and because the professor was relatively new they didn t know that i knew the bare minimum ie how to copy someone else using scikit learn biggest lie i claimed i had no obligations or plans during the summer the truth was if i didn t get paid i actually didn t give an f i would spend all of my time that summer grinding leetcode doing quant problems working out and possibly finding a way to get paid which is exactly what i ended up doing when i learned that the internship wasn t paid my mom wasn t very happy and she kept on insisting that i get a job i applied to a few and ended up being a tutor my task for the internship if i recall correctly was to find anomalies in a supercomputer using some sort of machine learning algorithm i was to communicate with the professor and one of his ph d students via slack and talk to his ph d student over zoom weekly and him biweekly though the professor did cancel and reschedule a lot of meetings i spent most of the first month reading a bunch of research papers reading those papers was actually really difficult for me to understand as my knowledge of systems and machine learning was pretty limited i guess if i had spent around hours a week looking up all the foreign concepts mentioned immersing myself with all the material it would actually start to make sense but i just didn t have the motivation to do it i just read the papers and if there were any buzzwords that i didn t understand i would just jot them down and look them up online so that when i had my weekly meeting with the ph d student i wouldn t appear to be as clueless as i actually was i usually would cram all to required readings in a day and call it a day late may to late june after a while i finally had access to the database but due to some confusion as to which database to analyze and because i forgot basic shell commands due to not using it for any cs classes for over a year yea i m stupid i was afk for a week there were some technical issues as well but let s just say that when the professor and i had a meeting he was very angry with the fact that i wasted an entire week s worth of work the ph d student had a hour zoom meeting with me after to clarify a lot of things and thanks to him i was able to get on the right track early july this period was ok but i d still consider it rather unproductive a lot of the work i was trying to do ended up being too data science related rather than machine learning related i found out that some of the work i was doing was already done by other people working on the same database i essentially just wrote python code in a jupyter notebook where i looked for patterns and graphed random things together there was an emphasis on time series data so i plotted things with time the professor and the ph d student didn t complain at this time in front of my face at least and it felt rather chill i did make some really stupid mistakes but they were rather trivial mid july to early august when it got to early august i ended up having a lot of work to do i had to spend a good amount of time tutoring people and unfortunately i didn t have the courtesy to tell the professor and the ph d student that i was busy i ended up taking a day hiatus which was the biggest mistake of my life as soon as i had that meeting with them the professor blatantly called me out and said i didn t do much and that my work was less than satisfactory i ended up taking what he said very personally and i ended up canceling all my other side hustles just to focus on the work being done i ended up developing a method using isolation forests to detect anomalies from a procstat database i was willing to apply this method to all the other databases but the internship ended the ph d student ironically thought my work during the last weeks of the internship was good and said i should ask the professor for a letter of recommendation fyi when i emailed the professor about it the man completely destroyed my ego and possibly deleted the entire github repository i worked on looking back i actually didn t do anything after taking computer architecture machine learning and operating systems classes i looked back at some of the work i had done and saw just how much of a baffoon i was one of the things i did was report that a cpu had an anomaly because it had too many context switches even though it was assigned a lot of processes the isolation forest algorithm i used also wasn t very accurate essentially i was just randomly playing around with data and made graphs and machine learning algorithms that would easily trigger anyone who at least knew the basics of systems let alone a person with a ph d in cs who specializes in high performance computing so the professor was rightfully pissed and i really should have put in the hours a week even if it wasn t paid i think after this experience taking credits last fall and failing miserably and going to therapy i ended up just deleting everything on my resume that i thought was remotely fake which resulted in it basically having class projects and a side project i did over the summer i m beginning to feel like this is not the move either since before i was able to at least get a get to know you interview but now my resume is so empty i practically appear to have no cs experience outside of school my mom also isn t really happy since i ended up not getting any offers and she really wanted to know why i even deleted a lot of good projects in the first place anyways i want to know what y all s take on this is tldr i didn t do too much for an unpaid internship last summer though i was able to learn a few things about high performance computing the professor i worked under fired me and possibly deleted all of my work is it still worth putting the experience on my resume despite the fact that i don t have much']

Representative_Docs: ['it is possible to make and actually pretty doable even for a year old guy the easiest approach is first to compile such data set of different facts by hand you ll pick a keyword and then list different facts under that then another keyword and again a list of different facts and go over as many of keywords as you want in the end you ll have a list of keywords and a bigger list of facts related to these now how to store such data set one option is to keep them in a database you can read about mysql and or postgresql these are the two more common database systems you will need to learn some sql for setting up database tables for filling these tables with keywords and facts and for querying the facts from these tables via one or another keyword for querying the facts you will need to set up some sort of back end side for your website it can be in php python javascript java net or others you can pick any of these really maybe php and python would be easier choices for a beginner i would suggest then to pick python another option is to store that data set in csv comma separated values format basically in a sort of text file then again with some back end programming language you will read in that csv file and return asked information facts then an option is to use text files all the rest is same as it was with csv then the easiest approach is to store this information in your front end side with javascript in map or in object you ll hard code that information into your javascript side and then retrieve it from map object based on a keyword you will need then no back end html css and javascript will be enough for you then the difficult path you ll build some sort of data mining and machine learning algorithm that goes over the data set facts and learns them also it will learn from the keywords that users are typing into your website and then tries to retrieve most suitable result basically something similar to google a search engine and unless you really want to spend a lot of time and i mean a lot on it better pick the easiest approach and keep your data inside javascript code as i mentioned in previous approach', 'as you are most likely more proficient than me in an art history i did study it during my high school years then you probably know the more used keywords and can limit the number of words from millions to perhaps hundred or two let s see different colors more famous artist names well known art styles different painting styles acrylic vs oil for example more famous art pieces and that s pretty much about it a person who is searching for an information is usually aware of what he is searching for for example when i wish to search for art styles in the beginning of th century then i will type in th century art styles not cereals or i like turtles like that you can eliminate all kind of words that have absolutely no relevance to art history or to art in general maybe what you can do is googling art history key words and look what it offers to you the search results that you get is already optimized by google itself so you do not need to create another search engine but use the information that an existing search engine can offer to you it has done all kind of analysis and statistics on search terms used in google when looking for art history key words phrase and will return the more common ones to you like that you can reduce the number from thousands to hundred or so the key words that you find to be relevant and then the key words that google is suggesting you sure you will not be able to cover of the art history keywords and of the facts but that is not your goal isn t it if you really want to improve your web site further then you can learn machine learning and data mining and learn to calculate the of match between the search phrase key word and between the fact but as it requires a whole new topic to be learned machine learning and data mining then i did not offer it to you as the approach you should choose right now i have studied both machine learning and data mining and they were really difficult courses in university but what you are trying to do can be done without that just by limiting the number of words from one million to two hundred for example and you can do the typing in word or something no need to write it down by hand like that it is easier to copy paste facts from one place to another', 'hello i am a second year cs student who just finished an introductory ds amp a course and our final project dealt with machine learning and nlp i found it Name: 33_interview_questions_interviews_interviewer
Representative_Docs: ['i just finished my first interview a couple days ago and let me say it was nothing like i expected it was more like a chat i guess i really knocked it out of the park because the interviewer seemed really keen on what i was talking about and he made great comments on the depth of some questions that i asked him in the end we chatted for minutes and he thanked me for taking time to probe into information related to the university if there s any improvement though even though i can converse in english i tend to stutter a lot when i m nervous and my east asian accent really pops out i hope the interviewer doesn t mind that because my conversations sound a little awkward because of the accent disclaimer i m not an ao nor an interviewer so i can t say for sure how my performance this time would positively negatively affect my admissions nor do i know anything about the admissions process i m speaking from experience as an applicant on what i ve learnt from this interview and what i wish i d have known sooner i prepared for the interview by looking through the a c wiki on interviews and college essay guy s college interview questions form top universities i also brainstormed potential interview questions and put them onto quizlet flashcards then filmed myself responding to the questions but alright advice know who you are and why you want to go to that university in particular interview questions always circulate around the same topics of why university why this major what are your interests etc etc etc while you don t have to write out a whole script for the interview i mean you shouldn t that s unnecessary you should have a clear sense of your personal traits passions motivations as well as a clear sense of why you re picking this university prepare a handful of ecs that you could talk about in detail research about the specialities of the university especially clubs you d like to participate in institutes you d like to do research in etc you should be able to namedrop some specific traditions courses or student organisations creativity and leadership colleges love that think of a few incidents where you demonstrated creativity leadership or how you re gonna apply your creativity and leadership to impact the university or the community my interviewer asked me about my leadership style and well lets say i wasn t expecting it and was a bit tongue tied on that practice by speaking a lot especially if you re not too good at speaking not used to holding long dialogues in english like me talk to the mirror talk to yourself whatever just keep your mind and mouth running a while before the interview so you ll be fully accustomed to chatting nonstop when it happens don t stalk your interviewer or you can stalk your interview but don t do so obsessively made the mistake of googling my interviewer out of curiosity a few days prior and search results popped up about his research company and website plus a very serious looking headshot needless to say i was freaked out because i worried that it meant we re gonna have sophisticated discussions about science technology and whatnot this wasn t true at all he was extremely friendly and encouraging even when i started stumbling with words we only touched upon the subject of his career when he told me about his current job and i asked him to explain his research just out of curiosity for context his work s focused on ai technology while i m more into bioscience but i wanted to know what being a researcher is like i guess i m lucky to have been interviewed by someone with a somewhat similar career to what i plan to do so i could chat with them about their job as well but if that doesn t work in your case don t sweat it remember the interview is ultimately about you not anyone else in fact you needn t know who your interviewer is beforehand if that saves you some stress prepare at least questions to ask you d probably need less than that but you never know i personally prepared questions and improvised this has been reiterated plenty of times i know but asking questions is an equally important part of the interview to when you re giving out responses and this is the part where you can actually control don t ask obvious questions you d want the interviewer to have to sit down and think for a while before they d be able to come up with an answer or questions that call upon their personal experiences for instance my interviewer liked my questions on what value did you learn from the school that changed you personally and did the school meet your expectations or differed from it and importantly double check the interview time and your computer device especially needed when you re having an online interview and in a different timezone than your interviewer my interviewer and i were a whole hours apart but i didn t check for any technical issues and only when the interview started did i realise that the browser didn t support using the computer camera so in the end we only had an audio interview bit of a bummer since i tidied up my shelf and changed into a nice shirt just for that so please check if the functions of your computer are working that s all the suggestions i could come up with off the top of my head at this moment guess i d just share it with y all from the pov of a current hs senior who s applying to unis hope this could help some of you folks good luck to anyone taking interviews btw i m wishing you all the best', 'it is extremely important that you have good interview practice in the days before an interview this will help you to be prepared and feel confident especially if it is your first interview interview practice allows you to give a variety of answers to a variety of questions if practiced well it also allows you to prepare for any curveball questions which may be thrown at you here are some tips on how to practice for your next job interview know what you want it s important to know what type of job you re interviewing for as well as what kind of company you d like to work at this will help narrow down your research on different companies and industries that might be a good fit for you research the company if possible find out as much information about the company as possible before going in for an interview including their vision mission statements and values recent news articles and industry reports about them and any other information that might help you determine if the company is right for you or not get organized make sure you have all of your documents in order before going in for an interview including copies of your resume references and letters of recommendation if applicable as well as any other materials you may need during the interview e g copies of previous pay stubs keep this information organized in one place so that it s easy to access during an interview if needed practice answering questions with friends or family members beforehand with their permission practice answering questions in different ways with your friends or family members most common interview questions robert half you can also try mock interview websites if you are not getting comfortable with anyone in the beginning interviews are often like choose your own adventure books interviewers ask questions that can lead to multiple answers the key is to give thoughtful responses that show off your strengths and fit with what the company needs so before an interview write down several potential answers for each question think of your answer with examples of situations where you ve demonstrated certain traits or skills that the company is looking for practice makes perfect practice answering written interview questions until they roll off your tongue easily don t memorize them word for word but understand their meaning so that when asked an unexpected question you can still give a confident answer ask questions at the end of every interview it s customary for the interviewer to ask if you have any questions however this isn t always necessary if they ve already answered all of your questions during the course of the meeting itself if this happens ask if there is anything else that needs clarifying or if there is any additional information that could be helpful before making a decision on hiring you or not hiring you based on your qualifications for the position at hand if you make a good first impression there s more chance for advancement and if you make a bad first impression it can haunt you for weeks until you get another chance at impressing your interviewers so start preparing for an interview good luck', 'edit here s a link to how i used notion to prepare my interview notes a link to the notion notes is available in the description too if you found this useful please drop a subscribe it d mean the world to me organisation is ke i ve interviewed at google and deepmind uber facebook amazon for roles that lie under the data scientist umbrella and this is the typical interview construction theme i ve observed software engineering applied statistics machine learning data wrangling manipulation and visualisation now nobody is expecting some super graduate level competency in all of these topics but you need to know enough to convince your interviewer that you re capable of delivering if they offered you the job how much you need to know depends on the job spec but in this increasingly competitive market no knowledge is lost i recommend using notion to organise your job prep it s extremely versatile and enables you to utilise the spaced repetition and active recall principles to nail down learning and deploying key topics that come up time and time again in a data scientist interview ali abdaal has a great tutorial on note taking with notion to maximise your learning potential during the interview process i used to run through my notion notes over and over but in particular right before my interview this ensured that key topics and definitions were loaded into my working memory and i didn t waste precious time ummmmmm ing when hit with some question software engineering not all data scientist roles will grill you on the time complexity of an algorithm but all of these roles will expect you to write code data science isn t one job but a collection of jobs that attracts talent from a variety of industries including the software engineering world as such you re competing with guys that know the ins and outs of writing efficient code and i would recommend spending at least hours a day in the lead up to your interview practicing the following concepts arrays hash tables linked lists two pointer based algorithms string algorithms interviewers love these binary search divide and conquer algorithms sorting algorithms dynamic programming recursion do not learn the algorithms off by heart this approach is useless because the interviewer can question you on any variation of the algorithm and you will be lost instead learn the strategy behind how each algorithm works learn what computational and spatial complexity are and learn why they are so fundamental to building efficient code leetcode was my best friend during interview preparation and is well worth the per month in my opinion your interviewers only have so many algorithm questions to sample from and this website covers a host of algorithm concepts including companies that are likely or are known to have asked these questions in the past there s also a great community who discuss each problem in detail and helped me during the myriad of stuck moments i encountered leetcode has a lite version with a smaller question bank if the price tag is too steep as do hackerrank and geeksforgeeks which are other great resources what you should do is attempt each question even if it s a brute force approach that takes ages to run then look at the model solution and try to figure out what the optimal strategy is then read up what the optimal strategy is and try to understand why this is the optimal strategy ask yourself questions like why is quicksort o n average time complexity why do two pointers and one for loop make more sense than three for loops applied statistics data science has an implicit dependence on applied statistics and how implicit that will be depends on the role you ve applied for where do we use applied statistics it pops up just about anywhere where we need to organise interpret and derive insights from data i studied the following topics intensely during my interviews and you bet your bottom dollar that i was grilled about each topic descriptive statistics what distribution does my data follow what are the modes of the distribution the expectation the variance probability theory given my data follows a binomial distribution what is the probability of observing paying customers in click through events hypothesis testing forming the basis of any question on a b testing t tests anova chi squared tests etc regression is the relationship between my variables linear what are potential sources of bias what are the assumptions behind the ordinary least squares solution bayesian inference what are some advantages disadvantages vs frequentist methods if you think this is a lot of material you are not alone i was massively overwhelmed with the volume of knowledge expected in these kinds of interviews and the plethora of information on the internet that could help me two invaluable resources come to mind when i was revising for interviews introduction to probability and statistics an open course on everything listed above including questions and an exam to help you test your knowledge machine learning a bayesian and optimization perspective by sergios theodoridis this is more a machine learning text than a specific primer on applied statistics but the linear algebra approaches outlined here really help drive home the key statistical concepts on regression the way you re going to remember this stuff isn t through memorisation you need to solve as many problems as you can get your hands on glassdoor is a great repo for the sorts of applied stats questions typically asked in interviews the most challenging interview i had by far was with g research but i really enjoyed studying for the exam and their sample exam papers were fantastic resources when it came to testing how far i was getting in my applied statistics revision machine learning now we come to the beast the buzzword of our millennial era and a topic so broad that it can be easy to get so lost in revision that you want to give up the applied statistics part of this study guide will give you a very very strong foundation to get started with machine learning which is basically just applied applied statistics written in fancy linear algebra but there are certain key concepts that came up over and over again during my interviews here is a by no means exhaustive set of concepts organised by topic metrics classification confusion matrices accuracy precision recall sensitivity f score tpr tnr fpr fnr type i and type ii errors auc roc curves metrics regression total sum of squares explained sum of squares residual sum of squares coefficient of determination and its adjusted form aic and bic advantages and disadvantages of rmse mse mae mape bias variance tradeoff over under fitting k nearest neighbours algorithm and the choice of k in bias variance trade off random forests the asymptotic property curse of dimensionality model selection k fold cross validation l and l regularisation bayesian optimization sampling dealing with class imbalance when training classification models smote for generating pseudo observations for an underrepresented class class imbalance in the independent variables sampling methods sources of sampling bias measuring sampling error hypothesis testing this really comes under under applied statistics but i cannot stress enough the importance of learning about statistical power it s enormously important in a b testing regression models ordinary linear regression its assumptions estimator derivation and limitations are covered in significant detail in the sources cited in the applied statistics section other regression models you should be familiar with are deep neural networks for regression random forest regression xgboost regression time series regression arima sarima bayesian linear regression gaussian process regression clustering algorithms k means hierarchical clustering dirichlet process mixture models classification models logistic regression most important one revise well multiple regression xgboost classification support vector machines it s a lot but much of the content will be trivial if your applied statistics foundation is strong enough i would recommend knowing the ins and outs of at least three different classification regression clustering methods because the interviewer could always and has previously asked what other methods could we have used what are some advantages disadvantages this is a small subset of the machine learning knowledge in the world but if you know these important examples the interviews will flow a lot more smoothly data manipulation and visualisation what are some of the steps for data wrangling and data cleaning before applying machine learning algorithms we are given a new dataset the first thing you ll need to prove is that you can perform an exploratory data analysis eda before you learn anything realise that there is one path to success in data wrangling pandas the pandas ide when used correctly is the most powerful tool in a data scientists toolbox the best way to learn how to use pandas for data manipulation is to download many many datasets and learn how to do the following set of tasks as confidently as you making your morning cup of coffee one of my interviews involved downloading a dataset cleaning it visualising it performing feature selection building and evaluating a model all in one hour it was a crazy hard task and i felt overwhelmed at times but i made sure i had practiced building model pipelines for weeks before actually attempting the interview so i knew i could find my way if i got lost advice the only way to get good at all this is to practice and the kaggle community has an incredible wealth of knowledge on mastering edas and model pipeline building i would check out some of the top ranking notebooks on some of the projects out there download some example datasets and build your own notebooks get familiar with the pandas syntax data organisation there are three sure things in life death taxes and getting asked to merge datasets and perform groupby and apply tasks on said merged datasets pandas is incredibly versatile at this so please practice practice practice data profiling this involves getting a feel for the meta characteristics of the dataset such as the shape and description of numerical categorical and date time features in the data you should always be seeking to address a set of questions like how many observations do i have what does the distribution of each feature look like what do the features mean this kind of profiling early on can help you reject non relevant features from the outset such as categorical features with thousands of levels names unique identifiers and mean less work for you and your machine later on work smart not hard or something woke like that data visualisation here you are asking yourself what does the distribution of my features even look like a word of advice if you didn t learn about boxplots in the applied statistics part of the study guide then here is where i stress you learn about them because you need to learn how to identify outliers visually and we can discuss how to deal with them later on histograms and kernel density estimation plots are extremely useful tools when looking at properties of the distributions of each feature we can then ask what does the relationship between my features look like in which case python has a package called seaborn containing very nifty tools like pairplot and a visually satisfying heatmap for correlation plots handling null values syntax errors and duplicate rows columns missing values are a sure thing in any dataset and arise due to a multitude of different factors each contributing to bias in their own unique way there is a whole field of study on how best to deal with missing values and i once had an interview where i was expected to know individual methods for missing value imputation in much detail check out this primer on ways of handling null values syntax errors typically arise when our dataset contains information that has been manually input such as through a form this could lead us to erroneously conclude that a categorical feature has many more levels than are actually present because hot hot hot n are all considered unique levels check out this primer on handling dirty text data finally duplicate columns are of no use to anyone and having duplicate rows could lead to overrepresentation bias so it s worth dealing with them early on standardisation or normalisation depending on the dataset you re working with and the machine learning method you decide to use it may be useful to standardize or normalize your data so that different scales of different variables don t negatively impact the performance of your model there s a lot here to go through but honestly it wasn t as much the memorise everything mentality that helped me insofar as it was the confidence building that learning as much as i could instilled in me i must have failed so many interviews before the formula clicked and i realised that all of these things aren t esoteric concepts that only the elite can master they re just tools that you use to build incredible models and derive insights from data best of luck on your job quest guys if you need any help at all please let me know and i will answer emails questions when i can']

Name: 35_medical_medicine_health_med
Representative_Docs: ['well many master degree courses are easier than undergrad especially if you aren t at a competitive school like say uc berkeley med school is easy for all my college friends who were hard science engineers like electrical chemical etc all told me the hardest part of med school was getting in and after the work was like high school level and just mainly memorization with understanding just interactions what master s degree and where did you get your medical degree my sister is a nurse and is more qualified than many doctors as she literally kills their confidences especially the young hot shots she easily should have gone to med school but actually didn t want to work in medicine for humans and wanted to be a vet but in vet school she developed a massive allergy to basically all animals that would constantly put her in shock so she just decided to do a year nursing program she also makes over k a year and does every bing from open heart brain nicu icu etc i have a cousin who is a md and he is bat shit stupid he took years to get into med school because he did so poorly on mcats and was a paramedic and volunteered at so many places and eventually was lucky enough some no name med school accepted him multi million dollar program means very little when i first started in engineering i was given a million dollar research grant on a team of basically myself with one very experienced engineer acting as a consultant on the project not trying to cut your wings and maybe you are a rockstar out of in big tech multibillion dollar rainmaker but just telling you what i have seen i am a phd non cs drop out from a top university and the thing i see the most generally is the most knowledgeable understand know how little they know and are more humble it s easy to put together front end work or make a simple machine learning feature with so many tools out but it s difficult to actually implement something innovative shit my year old nephew can code as well as many yoe swe folks of any background can become software engineers because software engineering has been broken down to be able to be implemented in small relative easy to pieces for the most part sure there are more complicated things but the vast majority of software engineering positions are very routine but the same can be said with many jobs', 'medical clinical informatics advanced so much in the last years they re applying machine learning and ai to medical records the algorithms for predicting clinical outcomes following medical interventions are becoming more advanced public health did not benefit from the significant investment in the hitech and affordable care acts you might have noticed that we don t even have a national database of covid vax recipients there are some other vaccine surveillance databases in some states there is nationalized reportable disease reporting for some infectious diseases most health departments do not access or contribute to electronic health records some health departments can access reports or dashboards in state health information exchanges following covid there has been venture capitalists investing in social needs referral platforms but there is currently no standardization for measuring or collecting data about the social determinants of health there is the gravity project which is working on some standards there is also the trusted exchange framework and common agreement which will accredit health information exchanges to be part of a new nation wide exchange designated by the cares act but there is significant opportunity right now to design new systems innovate and experiment the upside is that these systems have huge potential to improve health equity and social conditions which contribute to poor health and to demonstrate the need for funding in the social services sector or for reimbursing costs related to covering social services for medicare patients you can absolutely make a difference in this field right now i have a graduate certificate in public health informatics happy to answer any questions', 'i was in a similar situation a couple of years ago year old years into medical school after a couple of rotations and step i was pretty sure that i wanted out of being a doctor now i m a ml engineer at a faang company working remote and couldn t be happier here s my two cents completing residency in a non clinical specialty like radiology or pathology and a health informatics ds fellowship probably is the safest but longest path there are a few out there i think the path department at stanford has one that would set you up pretty well to work in a health tech company this of course would be a year plan before you even set foot into the industry and the roles that you d be transitioning into wouldn t actually be much dev work but probably act in a more consulting research role the benefit here is that you have a pretty sweet safety net being a licensed physician and the roles that you would be transitioning to would be compensated well for the amount of clinical training you ve endured compared to the average junior software dev job do some sort of coding bootcamp or complete a master s degree in health informatics biomedical engineering cs etc there are bootcamps like insight fellows that are specific to people with mds phds that i would probably be targeting i got into a master s program in the same uni as my med school and could transfer some credits to complete the degree in half the time i used my tech electives to specifically pick classes useful for my future career machine learning computer vision data structs amp algos etc my thesis also involved a lot of programming i think if you straight up just want to be a swe ds this is probably the route i would go since the technical degree helps a lot to get past the initial resume screening process i would also say that in the second route the md degree matters a lot less after landing your first job the only thing that really matters now is your performance during the interview process no one bothers to ask about your clinical experience i ve also worked with people that went the first route who were chief medical officers chief analytics officers of health tech companies and startups but do very little actual programming work so it really depends on what you want long term your biggest advantage is that you have options looking back i was very grateful that the med school gave me a two year hiatus to figure things out that allowed me to complete another degree get an internship and work full time for months before i fully committed to never going back to medical school you probably have a similar timeline before you really need to think about if you should go back to residency after completing your degree']

Name: 38_copilot_ide_code_github
Representative_Docs: ['so i think people going so far as saying notepad or a plain text editor are a bit much that said i do think it can be useful for some people in some cases to learn on lite ides which is to say those that do syntax highlight documentation look up and a couple of environment management features good examples of this are like the arduino ide idle and drracket vscode is a little complicated but also gets there depending on extension usage the reason for this is because they don t provide as much support they force people to actually learn how programming and the programming languages they are using actually work if i had never been forced to sit down with sublime text and a make file and actually use a c compiler by hand i wouldn t have understood how binaries and object files are put together ditto for python virtual environment and no package manager to learn how python s import system worked and so on for dozens of other systems whether this is actually useful for beginners or not is perhaps questionable but i would argue that the issue with some of the complicated ides is that they can do magic and you will have no clue what happened you ll sit there and hit rebuild or deleting random folders or checking out from version control just lost as to why anything went wrong that loss of control can be it s own reason but it can also make it hard to learn the actual rules if you go to another ide or even framework if the ide supports only specific portions of the language ecosystem and so to ensure beginners learn the actual language and not just random ide buttons it can be useful to reduce the noise that the ide brings especially when we consider modern ides with their ai code complete i shudder to imagine what fresh hell programming language tas are dealing with on that front', 'i have seen questions about what ide you should use for a specific purpose or language with many saying vs code is the way to go for most things others saying vim for productivity visual studio gets brought up into the mix hell when i was at my first coop and was coding in python the ai tech told me to use pycharm over other ide s or text editors but what makes a good ide what should people be looking for when choosing an ide when should people be using an ide vs a text editor what features do you personally look for can os have a factor in ide performance does ide choice really even matter thank you in advance for your responses the why behind picking a certain ide was never explained to me and i just used what was recommended to me in the past', 'you should try the following experiment find someone who has zero knowledge about programming sit them in front of a copilot enabled ide and ask them to build something like a reddit clone or a twitter clone without any assistance provide a high level set of requirements like being able to create accounts create posts and upvote others posts this type of project would be a typical portfolio project for someone going though a several month bootcamp my bet is that it will be a complete failure heck they might get stuck on step which is picking an appropriate language and creating files with the correct file extension to activate copilot copilot doesn t build complete applications and i think this is a critical difference between something that is just a tool vs something that is actually worrying copilot can write a function to reverse a linked list or find whether a string is a palindrome or whatever but that would be like of a real application and while you can invoke copilot times and generate snippets there s no guarantee that those snippets work with one another which is a big problem if you don t know how to program and can t actually evaluate what it s generating of course it s possible that one day we ll get ai advanced enough to build complete applications and maybe that day programmers will get worried but for now there are no indications we re anywhere close and until then copilot is just fancy autocomplete']
